finding: Moral valence of outcome affects attribution of epistemic states: subjects less likely to attribute knowledge when side-effect is beneficent than when harmful, contrary to pure epistemic theories of knowledge.

Folk moral intuitions shift with perspective distance: subjects endorse moral objectivism for same-culture judgments but progressively adopt relativist positions when considering radically different cultural frameworks.

Knowledge ascriptions are sensitive to implicit contrast arguments: agreement with knowledge claims differs based on which alternative is salient in context, supporting contrastive rather than binary theories of knowledge.

Incompatibilist intuitions about free will under determinism arise largely from confusion between determinism and bypassing of mental states; when bypassing is explicitly ruled out, most subjects endorse compatibilism.

Individual differences in inhibitory control predict L2 phonological perception accuracy but less consistently predict production accuracy, suggesting inhibition aids learning of category distinctions more than motor execution.

Stakes do not affect knowledge ascriptions when epistemic factors are held constant; apparent stakes effects in earlier studies reflect sensitivity to deontic modality or salience of alternatives rather than knowledge per se.
